questions.question: Let f: ℤ → ℤ be defined by f(x) = 3x + 2. Prove that f is injective but not surjective. Find the range of f.
keyPoints: Prove injectivity: Assume f(a)=f(b) ⇒ 3a+2=3b+2 ⇒ a=b
Show not surjective: Show an integer in codomain with no pre-image (e.g., 1 cannot be written as 3x+2 for integer x)

questions.question: Define functions f: A → B and g: B → C. Explain when (f ∘ g) exists and state two properties of function composition.
keyPoints: (f ∘ g) exists if domain of f equals codomain of g
Composition is associative: (f ∘ g) ∘ h = f ∘ (g ∘ h)
Composition is not commutative in general: f ∘ g ≠ g ∘ f
Identity element exists: f ∘ I = I ∘ f = f

questions.question: Find the inverse of the function f: ℝ → ℝ⁺ defined by f(x) = x² + 1. Is this function bijective? Justify your answer.
keyPoints: Let y = x² + 1, solve for x: x = ±√(y-1)
Since x has two possible values, inverse is not a function
Not bijective because not injective (f(2)=f(-2)=5)
To make bijective, restrict domain to [0,∞) or (-∞,0]

questions.question: Let f: {1,2,3} → {a,b,c} be defined as f(1)=a, f(2)=b, f(3)=c. Find f⁻¹. Is f bijective?
keyPoints: f is bijective because it is both injective (all images distinct) and surjective (all codomain elements used)
Since f is bijective, inverse exists as a function from {a,b,c} to {1,2,3}

questions.question: Explain the floor function ⌊x⌋ and ceiling function ⌈x⌉ with examples. Compute ⌊2.7⌋ + ⌈-2.7⌉.
keyPoints: Floor: greatest integer ≤ x; ⌊2.7⌋ = 2, ⌊-2.7⌋ = -3
Ceiling: smallest integer ≥ x; ⌈2.7⌉ = 3, ⌈-2.7⌉ = -2

questions.question: Given f(x) = 2x + 1 and g(x) = x² - 1, find: (i) f ∘ g (ii) g ∘ f (iii) f ∘ f
State and prove the necessary and sufficient condition for a function to have an inverse.
keyPoints: A function f: A → B has an inverse f⁻¹: B → A iff f is bijective
Proof (necessity): If f⁻¹ exists, then f must be injective (otherwise f⁻¹ would not be single-valued) and surjective (every y in B must have pre-image for f⁻¹ to be defined)
Proof (sufficiency): If f is bijective, define f⁻¹(y) = unique x such that f(x)=y. This is a valid function and satisfies inverse properties.

questions.question: Discuss all types of functions with respect to injectivity and surjectivity. For each type, give an example from ℝ → ℝ and explain whether inverse exists.
keyPoints: Four types: (i) Neither injective nor surjective - f(x)=x² (not injective over ℝ, not surjective as negative outputs missing)
(ii) Injective but not surjective - f(x)=eˣ (strictly increasing, but negative reals not in range)
(iii) Surjective but not injective - f(x)=sin(x) (every value in [-1,1] attained infinitely many times)
(iv) Bijective - f(x)=x³ (strictly monotonic, onto ℝ, inverse x^(1/3))
Only bijective functions have inverses that are also functions

questions.question: Let f: ℤ → ℤ be defined by f(n) = 2n + 1. (a) Is f injective? Justify. (b) Is f surjective onto ℤ? If not, find the range. (c) Can we define an inverse function? If yes, find it; if not, how can we modify the function to make it bijective?
keyPoints: (a) f is injective: if 2a+1 = 2b+1 ⇒ a=b
(b) f is not surjective: odd integers only in range; even integers like 2 have no pre-image; Range = {2k+1 | k ∈ ℤ} = all odd integers
(c) No inverse exists as function; to make bijective: (i) restrict codomain to odd integers, OR (ii) define g: ℤ → ℤ where g(n)=⌊n/2⌋ but this is not true inverse
Alternative: define h: ℤ → ℤ where h(2k)=k, h(2k+1)=k - this is surjective but not injective

questions.question: Prove that: (a) Composition of two injective functions is injective. (b) Composition of two surjective functions is surjective. (c) Give counterexamples to show that injectivity of composition does not imply injectivity of individual functions.
keyPoints: (a) Let f,g be injective, (f∘g)(a)=(f∘g)(b) ⇒ f(g(a))=f(g(b)) ⇒ g(a)=g(b) ⇒ a=b (since g injective) ⇒ f injective
(b) Let f,g be surjective. For any z in codomain of f∘g, since f is surjective, ∃y such that f(y)=z. Since g is surjective, ∃x such that g(x)=y. Then (f∘g)(x)=f(g(x))=f(y)=z

(c) Counterexample: f(x)=x² from ℝ≥0→ℝ≥0 is injective but g(x)=√x from ℝ≥0→ℝ≥0 is not injective? Actually g∘f: ℝ≥0→ℝ≥0 with g∘f(x)=x is bijective but f is not injective: let f(x)=x² (not injective over ℝ), g(x)=√x (injective over ℝ≥0), then f∘g(x)=x but f is not injective.

questions.question: (a) Define one-to-one and onto functions with examples. (b) If A has m elements and B has n elements, find the number of: (i) functions from A to B (ii) one-to-one functions from A to B (iii) onto functions from A to B when m = n.
keyPoints: (a) One-to-one: f(x₁)=f(x₂)⇒x₁=x₂, example f(x)=2x from ℝ→ℝ; Onto: ∀y∈B,∃x∈A such that f(x)=y, example f(x)=x³ from ℝ→ℝ
(b)(i) nᵐ (each of m elements has n choices)
(b)(ii) P(n,m) = n!/(n-m)! when n≥m, else 0
(b)(iii) When m=n=n, number of onto functions = n! (bijective functions, all permutations)
